results.possibleIdentity: David Glasser is a filmmaker, technical director, and multimedia artist whose work is closely associated with the historical intersection of art and technology. He is known for his role as a technical director and collaborator with pioneering video artist Nam June Paik, specifically credited with working on technical scores for Paik and other experimental media artists. Glasser was active in the late 1960s and 1970s, a period marked by his association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, where he contributed to documenting and facilitating the organization's mission of fostering collaborations between artists and engineers. His involvement in the art world continued into the 1980s, where he served as the Technical Director for New Music America '87 in Philadelphia, a festival that showcased pop art, video art, and earth art. In contemporary contexts, he is frequently cited in discussions and archival screenings related to the history of E.A.T., such as those hosted by the Daiwa Anglo-Japanese Foundation and the Institute of Contemporary Arts (ICA).

David E. Glasser is an architect and academic who was active during the late 1960s and 1970s. His work often focused on the intersection of design, technology, and social issues, such as urban renewal and architectural education. He is documented as having visited and analyzed housing developments in international contexts like Quito during the 1960s. Glasser has written extensively on the evolution of architectural pedagogy, notably in the Journal of Architectural Education, reflecting on the transition from functionalist principles to intuitive aesthetic design. Given E.A.T.'s heavy reliance on architects and engineers for projects like the Pepsi Pavilion, he represents a possible identity of a professional from that era whose work overlapped with the organization's collaborative goals.

results.possibleIdentity: Bill Mullen was an experimental composer and violist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a member of the first graduating class (1967) of the University of California, San Diego (UCSD) Department of Music, which was established as a hub for experimental studies, composition, and the intersection of art and technology. Mullen is described as an experimental music pioneer alongside contemporaries like Alan Strange (a composer and visual artist) and Jack Logan. This group was part of a burgeoning avant-garde scene that explored computer music, electronic systems, and multimedia performances, aligning closely with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. E.A.T., co-founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg, promoted collaborations between artists and engineers, a focus that defined the curriculum and research at UCSD during Mullen's tenure there.

Bill V. Mullen is a prominent scholar and author who focuses on the cultural and political history of the 1960s and 1970s, particularly the Black Arts Movement and the intersections of African American and Asian American radical art. While primarily recognized as a professor and literary critic (formerly at the University of Texas at San Antonio and Purdue University), he has authored and edited numerous books such as 'Popular Fronts: Chicago and African-American Cultural Politics, 1935–1946' (1999) and 'Afro-Orientalism' (2004). His work frequently explores the historical identity of artists and activists from the late 1960s era, making him a central figure in the academic reconciliation of artist identities from that period.

results.possibleIdentity: Richard Serra (1938–2024) was a preeminent American sculptor and video artist whose career gained significant momentum in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Initially trained as a painter at Yale University, Serra shifted his focus to sculpture after moving to New York City in 1966. He is best known for his large-scale, site-specific installations that emphasize the physical properties of industrial materials like lead, rubber, and weathering steel (Corten steel). In 1967, he developed his 'Verb List,' a series of actions (such as 'to roll,' 'to splash,' 'to prop') that informed his process-oriented works, including his 'Splash' pieces (molten lead cast into wall-floor junctures) and 'Prop' pieces (lead plates balanced by gravity). Serra was also an early practitioner of video art, producing 'Hand Catching Lead' in 1968. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, notably participating in E.A.T.-sponsored projects that utilized modern technology to broadcast art, such as cable television series in the early 1970s alongside artists like Michael Snow and Andy Warhol.

results.possibleIdentity: Dr. Gary Williams is a theater historian, scholar, and artist who served as a Professor Emeritus of Drama at the Catholic University of America (CUA). He was active in the theater community starting in the late 1960s and early 1970s, joining the faculty at CUA in 1974 and retiring in 2002. His work is associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through archival documentation held at the Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden, specifically within the records of the exhibition 'Artistic Collaboration in the Twentieth Century' (1984). These records include a folder for 'Gary Williams (Catholic University)' containing 'Photographs of Production,' situated alongside materials related to E.A.T. and other collaborative art movements. Additionally, he served as the editor of 'Theatre Survey,' a prominent academic journal for theater history.

Gary Williams (1951–2015) was a professional engineer and set designer active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He spent 32 years at Motorola, where he contributed to the development of several new technologies, including the digital watch and Charge-Coupled Device (CCD) technology used in video cameras. Parallel to his engineering career, he was a prolific artist and 'tireless volunteer set designer' for over 35 years, primarily within the Phoenix Union High School District's Theatre Arts Departments. His background as both an engineer and a visual/theatrical artist aligns with the collaborative mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which fostered partnerships between corporate engineers (from companies like Motorola and Bell Labs) and artists during the 1960s and 1970s.

Gary Williams is a renowned Gumbaynggirr and Bundjalung Aboriginal activist, actor, and radio announcer who has been a prominent figure in Australian political and artistic circles since the 1960s. He was a key participant in the 1970 Osaka Expo (Expo '70) as part of the Aboriginal Theatre Foundation. This event is significant in the history of technology and art as it was the site of the Pepsi Pavilion, the major immersive project produced by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Williams has also been involved in major political actions such as the 1965 Freedom Ride and the 1972 Aboriginal Tent Embassy.

results.possibleIdentity: Gary Stephan (born 1942) is an American abstract painter, sculptor, and printmaker who became a prominent figure in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in Brooklyn, he studied industrial design at the Parsons School of Design and Pratt Institute before earning his Master of Fine Arts from the San Francisco Art Institute in 1967. Upon returning to New York, he served as a studio assistant to Jasper Johns. Stephan's early professional career was marked by significant exhibitions at the David Whitney Gallery (1970) and the Whitney Biennial (1971 and 1973). New York Times critic Roberta Smith described his work as being among the most closely watched developments of the early 1970s. His practice is characterized by a postmodern approach to abstraction, often experimenting with unconventional materials and the mechanics of pictorial space. For instance, his early work involved pouring resin into frameworks to create paintings where the material itself served as the support. His work is included in major permanent collections, such as the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, and the Whitney Museum of American Art. He has also been a longtime faculty member in the MFA program at the School of Visual Arts (SVA) in New York. While primarily known as a painter, his experimental approach and association with the experimental art circles of the late 1960s (documented by photographers Shunk-Kender, who also recorded E.A.T. projects) align him with the period's exploration of new materials and processes.

results.possibleIdentity: Bernard Childs (1910–1985) was an American painter, printmaker, and sculptor who was a pioneer in integrating industrial technology with traditional art forms. Born in Brooklyn and active in both New York and Paris, he is best known for his development of the "power drypoint" technique, in which he used electric power tools like drills and routers to engrave metal plates for printmaking. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, he expanded his experimentation into "light sculptures," which consisted of sheets of transparent acrylic (Plexiglas) that were engraved with symbols and illuminated from below or within. This work with light and machines aligned him with the broader art and technology movement of the era. A major retrospective of his 1960s paintings, prints, and light sculptures was held at the Storm King Art Center in 1969. While often associated with the same artistic milieu as Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) due to his use of electronics and industrial tools, he is distinct from the dancer Lucinda Childs, who was a prominent member of that organization. He lived and worked at the Hotel Chelsea in New York from 1966 until his death in 1985.

results.possibleIdentity: Arlene Haller was a New Jersey-based sculptor and artist active during the late 1960s and 1970s. She was notably involved in the pioneering Mary H. Dana Women Artists Series at Douglass College, Rutgers University, where she participated in the 1977/1978 (Year 7) exhibition season as part of a group show titled "New Jersey Women Artists." During this time, she was a contemporary of several figures central to the intersection of art and technology, such as Lillian Schwartz. Haller also exhibited her sculpture in juried shows at the Visual Arts Center of New Jersey (formerly the Summit Art Center), including a 1975 exhibition. Her practice coincided with the height of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which had a strong presence in the New Jersey and New York art scenes through collaborations with engineers at institutions like Bell Labs. She is listed in the Rutgers archival records as one of the artists whose work contributed to the representation and recognition of women in contemporary art during the 1970s.
